name: walnut brie salad
instructions:
Make the strawberry peach vinaigrette per its own recipe.

Build the salad: arrange a bed of mixed greens (spring mix). Layer in chunks of brie and crumbled goat cheese — both work together; pick one or use both. Top with the strawberry peach vinaigrette — spoon it on rather than pour it (the dressing is pulpy and thick by design).

Finish with toasted walnuts for crunch.
notes:
Salad built around brie and goat cheese as the structural cheese pair, with toasted walnuts for texture and the strawberry peach vinaigrette as the flavor-carrying dressing. The vinaigrette does most of the heavy lifting; the rest is a vehicle for it.

CHEESE — BRIE AND GOAT CHEESE (NOT MOZZARELLA): brie's creamy richness and goat cheese's sharp tang work together — pick one or layer both. Mozzarella is a clash here: it's too mild and doesn't carry against the walnuts or the sweet-fruit dressing. (Mozzarella is the right call for the standard caprese with tomato vinaigrette; this isn't that salad.)

TOASTED WALNUTS, NOT RAW: toasting brings out the nutty depth that balances the sweet fruit dressing. Raw walnuts read tannic against the sweetness. A quick dry-toast in a pan for a few minutes is enough.

WHY SPRING MIX (MIXED GREENS): the salad needs a green base that brings bitterness/structure but stays out of the dressing's way. Per the herbs and greens article, mixed greens (baby arugula mix) are the right call — calibrated bitterness without overpowering. Romaine or iceberg would read too neutral; full-grown arugula would compete with the dressing.
